attributes.stem:
An 84-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department from her nursing home with a three-day history of poor oral intake and increasing confusion. She has a medical history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and Alzheimer dementia. Her regular medications include furosemide, perindopril, metformin, and donepezil. She has recently recovered from a five-day episode of diarrhoea.

On examination, she appears lethargic. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Her mucous membranes are dry, and she has reduced skin turgor. Her capillary refill time is three seconds. Her chart reports a baseline weight of 45kg.

Blood tests on arrival to ED reveal:

Sodium: 162 mmol/L (135-145)
Potassium: 3.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)
Urea: 15.2 mmol/L (3.0-8.0)
Creatinine: 145 µmol/L (45-90)
Glucose: 7.2 mmol/L (3.5-7.8)

After commencing appropriate initial fluid replacement with sodium chloride 0.45% at 125ml/hr 2 hours ago, there is a slight reduction in the serum sodium level to 161 mmol/L (135-145).

You estimate her free water deficit to be approximately 3.2 litres.
attributes.stemQuestion: In addition to 4-hourly sodium monitoring, which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Continue current fluid at the current rate
Increase to sodium chloride 0.45% at 200 mL/hour
Switch to sodium chloride 0.9% at 125 mL/hour
Decrease to sodium chloride 0.45% at 80 mL/hour
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with hypernatraemia (sodium 162 mmol/L) in the context of recent diarrhoea, poor oral intake, and loop diuretic use. The examination findings are consistent with significant dehydration. The elevated urea and creatinine indicate acute kidney injury, likely pre-renal due to volume depletion.

Continue current fluid rate is the most appropriate next step. The current approach with 0.45% sodium chloride at 125 mL/hour has already demonstrated an appropriate initial response with a slight reduction in sodium from 162 to 161 mmol/L after 2 hours. This gradual correction is ideal, as the rate of sodium correction should not exceed 10 mmol/L in 24 hours to prevent cerebral oedema. Regular monitoring allows for careful titration of the fluid rate to achieve this target correction rate while addressing the volume deficit.

Switch to 5% dextrose at 125 mL/hour would provide free water without sodium, which could potentially correct the hypernatraemia more rapidly. However, this approach risks too rapid correction and could exacerbate the volume depletion by causing an osmotic diuresis in this patient with diabetes if hyperglycaemia develops.

Increase to sodium chloride 0.45% at 200 mL/hour would risk too rapid correction of the sodium level. The current rate is already achieving an appropriate correction pace, and increasing the rate could lead to cerebral oedema and neurological complications.

Switch to sodium chloride 0.9% at 125 mL/hour would be inappropriate as isotonic saline has a higher sodium concentration than 0.45% saline. This would slow the correction of hypernatraemia and might even worsen it, as the sodium concentration in 0.9% saline (154 mmol/L) is close to the patient's current sodium level.

Decrease to sodium chloride 0.45% at 80 mL/hour would slow the correction rate unnecessarily. The current correction rate is appropriate and not too rapid, so decreasing the rate would delay resolution of the hypernatraemia and prolong the patient's dehydration.

The management of hypernatraemia requires a careful balance between correcting the free water deficit and avoiding rapid changes in serum osmolality. The calculation of free water deficit and selection of appropriate fluids (hypotonic solutions like 0.45% sodium chloride or 5% dextrose) are important considerations. The underlying causes, including the recent diarrhoea and ongoing furosemide therapy, should also be addressed as part of the comprehensive management plan.
learningPoints: Initial management of hypernatraemia focuses on careful fluid replacement to avoid rapid correction which can cause cerebral oedema.
The rate of sodium correction should not exceed 10 mmol/L in 24 hours.
Regular monitoring of serum sodium levels is essential to guide fluid therapy and prevent complications.
references: Brennan M, Mulkerrin L, O'Keeffe ST, O'Shea PM. Approach to the Management of Hypernatraemia in Older Hospitalised Patients. J Nutr Health Aging. 2021;25(10):1161-1166. doi: 10.1007/s12603-021-1692-5. PMID: 34866143. Available online at ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1279770723007418#cesec110

Yun G, Baek SH, Kim S. Evaluation and management of hypernatremia in adults: clinical perspectives. Korean J Intern Med. 2023;38(3):290-302. doi:10.3904/kjim.2022.346. Available online at ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10175862/
Stern R. Treatment of hypernatremia in adults [Internet]. UpToDate. 2024. Available from: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/treatment-of-hypernatremia-in-adults
Naganathan S, Al-Dhahir MA. Hypernatremia [Internet]. Nih.gov. StatPearls Publishing; 2023. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441960/

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree

attributes.name: Level III
attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
